description: Inside a coffee shop, Chandler and Rachel sit on a couch. Chandler reads a newspaper and sighs, shaking his head. Rachel holds a cup and looks toward him.
Rachel raises one hand slightly and says, "I don't know."
Chandler looks up from his paper, responding, "What? Well, as old as he is in dog years do you think Snoopy should still be allowed to fly this thing?"
Gunther walks up to the couch holding a notepad.
Gunther looks at Rachel and says, "Rachel?"
Rachel turns her head toward him, saying, "Yeah?" Gunther asks, "You remember when you first came here how you spent two weeks getting trained by another waitress?"
Rachel smiles and nods, replying, "Oh, sure. Do"
Rachel points to herself, finishing, "you need me to train somebody new?"
Gunther laughs briefly.
Gunther stops laughing and says, "Good one."
Rachel's smile fades as she watches him.
Gunther points his pen at her and says, "Actually, uh, Terry wants you to take the training again. Whenever." He turns and walks away.
Rachel stares in Gunther's direction with her mouth slightly open. Chandler glances from Gunther to Rachel.
Rachel turns to Chandler and asks, "Do you believe that?"
Chandler looks at Rachel with a neutral expression, then looks back down at his newspaper. Rachel keeps her gaze fixed on him.
Chandler looks back at Rachel and says, "Yeah." The scene turns completely dark.
The screen remains entirely dark.
Outside, an apartment building is shown with falling snow. The setting then changes to an indoor hallway where a man hands money to a young girl in a brown uniform holding two boxes.
The girl looks up at the man and says, "So that's two boxes of the Holiday Macaroons. On behalf of the Brownbirds America, I salute you." She blows a small whistle hanging from her neck and salutes.
The girl lowers her hand and walks past the man, proceeding down the hallway.
Ross and Chandler walk up a staircase. Ross, holding a tennis racket, says, "Just admit it, Chandler. You have no backhand."
Chandler gestures with his hand and replies, "Excuse me, little one. I have a very solid backhand."
Ross mimics a protective gesture and says, "Shielding your face and shrieking like a girl is not a backhand."
Chandler stops on the stairs and turns to face Ross.
Chandler says, "I was shrieking like a Marine."
Ross steps up beside Chandler, holding the tennis racket ready.
Ross grips the racket and says, "All right, here. Watch me execute the three P's of championship play."
Ross assumes a swinging stance.
Ross swings the racket, stating, "Power,"
Ross prepares for another swing.
Ross swings again, saying, "precision," then swings a third time, continuing, "shook and panache." The racket hits something off-screen, followed by a loud thud and a young girl's scream.
Ross and Chandler freeze, their eyes wide as they look down the stairs. The scene changes to the coffee shop where Monica and Phoebe sit on the couch.
Monica looks across the table and says, "You broke a little girl's leg."
Ross sits on the arm of the couch and says, "I know. I feel horrible, okay?"
Chandler, sitting at a table with a newspaper, remarks, "Says here that a Muppet got whacked on Sesame Street last night."
Chandler looks up from his newspaper toward Ross.
Chandler asks Ross, "Where exactly were you? Around tennis."
Rachel walks up behind Ross and rests her hands on his shoulders.
Ross says, "I'm going to go see her. I want to bring you something. What do you think she'd like?"
Monica suggests, "Maybe a Hello Kitty doll?" Phoebe adds, "The ability to walk?"
Ross turns his head slightly. Rachel smiles, pats his shoulder, and removes her hands.
Rachel points away and says, "I'm going to get back to Retraining."
Ross stands up and says, "All right, see you guys."
Ross begins walking toward the door as Chandler watches him.
Chandler calls out loudly, "Look out, kids. He's coming."
Ross exits the coffee shop. Joey walks in through the door wearing a jacket and checks his watch as he approaches the seating area.
Joey looks toward the group and says, "And I got to go sell some Christmas trees."
Phoebe, holding a mug, replies, "Have fun. Oh, wait, no, don't. I forgot. I am totally against that now," pointing her finger.
Joey stops walking, turns around, and gestures with both hands.
Joey asks, "What, me having a job?" while taking off his jacket.
Joey places his jacket over the back of a chair.
Phoebe gestures broadly and says, "No. I am against innocent trees being cut down in their prime and their corpses grotesquely dressed in,"
Phoebe waves her fingers, finishing, "like, tinsel and twinkly lights."
Joey leans forward slightly, staring at Phoebe.
Phoebe asks, "Hey, how do you sleep at night?" Joey replies, "Well, I'm pretty tired from lugging the trees around."
Chandler tilts his head and winces slightly. Joey sits down on the arm of the couch.
Joey gestures with his hands toward Phoebe and says, "Hey, Phoebe, listen. You got this all wrong."
Joey holds his hands in front of him.
Joey points his finger, stating, "Those trees were born to be Christmas trees. They're fulfilling their life purpose by making people happy."
Phoebe looks at him and asks, "Really?"
Joey gives a slight nod while maintaining eye contact with Phoebe.
In a coffee shop, Chandler sits with a newspaper folded on his knee.
The setting changes to Joey and Phoebe sitting on a couch. Joey nods and says, "Yes."
Joey gestures with both hands as he continues, "And the trees are happy, too, because for most of them, it's their only chance to see New York." Phoebe listens with her hands resting on her lap.
The setting changes to the coffee shop counter. Gunther holds a black tray in both hands while speaking to Rachel.
Gunther tells her, "And after you've delivered the drinks, you take the empty tray." Rachel covers her face with one hand, then lowers it, saying, "Gunther, please."

Rachel gestures outward, stating, "I have worked here for two and a half years. I know the empty trays. Go over there." She points to her right. Gunther places the tray on the counter and asks, "What if you put them here?"
Gunther keeps his hand resting on the tray. Rachel looks at the tray.

Rachel points at the tray and says, "Well, you know, that's actually a really good idea because that way they'll be closer to the mugs." She gestures with her thumb toward her shoulder. "You know what? You should have the other"
Rachel finishes her sentence, "waitresses do that, too."
Gunther looks steadily at Rachel.
Gunther replies, "They already do."
Rachel's expression drops and she looks away, lowering her gaze.

Gunther says, "That's why they call it the tray spot." Rachel looks back at him, bringing her hands up near her chest, and replies, "Jeez, I always heard him talk about that. I sort of just thought it was, like, a club they went to."
Rachel covers her face with both hands and says, "Oh, God,"
Rachel lowers her hands, crossing them against her chest.
Looking over her shoulder away from Gunther, Rachel says, "I'm sorry."
Gunther looks at her profile.
Gunther says, "It's all right,"
Gunther lowers his gaze.
Looking down, Gunther mutters softly, "sweetheart."

Gunther reaches for an orange coffee pot. The setting changes to a dimly lit bedroom decorated with space posters. Ross sits on a chair next to a bed where Sarah, a young girl with her leg in a cast, sits holding a plush toy.
Ross leans forward, resting his hands on his knees. "So, this must be kind of neat for you. Your dad tells me you get a couple of days off school and you"
Ross continues, "don't have to sell those cookies anymore."
Sarah looks at him.
Sarah replies, "Well, I kind of wanted to sell the cookies. The girl who sells most wins a trip to Space Camp and gets to sit in a real space shuttle."
Ross raises his eyebrows and says, "Wow,"
Ross picks up a plush space shuttle toy from the bed.
Holding the toy in both hands, Ross says, "you really like all this space, huh?"
Sarah says, "Yeah. My dad says if I spend as much time helping him clean apartments as I do daydreaming about outer space he'd be able to afford"
Sarah continues, "a trip to the Taj Mahal." Ross responds, "I think you'd have to clean a whole lot of apartments to go all the way to India." Sarah shakes her head and says, "No. The one"
Sarah finishes, "Atlantic City. Dad loves the slot."
Ross gives a slight laugh and nods.
Sarah adds, "He says he's going to double the college money my grandma left me."
Ross's smile fades and his mouth drops open slightly.
Ross swallows and says, "Well, good luck to dad."
Ross leans forward again.
Ross asks, "Say, how many more boxes would you have to sell in order to win?"
Sarah answers, "The girl who won last year sold 475."
Ross says, "Yeah?" Sarah replies, "So far I've sold 75"
Ross squints slightly, looking thoughtful.
Ross says, "400."
Ross shifts his posture on the chair.
Ross says, "Well, that sounds doable."
Ross reaches his right hand inside his leather jacket.
Ross asks, "How much are the boxes?"
Sarah answers, "$5 a box." Ross pulls his hand out of his jacket, holding a black wallet.
Ross asks, "And what is second prize?"
Sarah looks down briefly, thinking.
Sarah says, "A ten speed bike. But I'd rather have something my dad couldn't sell."
Ross freezes, his eyes widening slightly as he holds his wallet.
Ross nods and says, "Well, that makes sense."
Sarah looks at him expectantly.
Sarah asks, "Do you do me one favor, if it's not too much trouble?"
Ross maintains eye contact with her.
Ross answers softly, "Yeah, Sarah, anything."
Sarah requests, "Could you pull the curtains open for me? The astronauts from the space shuttle are going to be on the news. Since we don't have a"
Sarah continues, "TV the lady across the alley said she'd push hers up through a window so I can watch it."
Ross stares at her silently, his mouth slightly open, holding the wallet.
The setting changes to an exterior evening view of a city street. Cars drive along the road with their headlights on.
Pedestrians walk on the sidewalk past illuminated storefronts. Two tall skyscrapers are visible in the background against the dark sky.
In a hallway, Ross stands outside a closed door and leans toward the peephole. He says, "Yes, hi. I'm selling Brownbird cookies." An off-screen voice replies, "You're no brown bird."
The off-screen voice continues, "I can see you through my peephole." Ross's eyes widen slightly.
Ross gestures to his chest with his thumb, keeping his gaze on the peephole.
Ross points to a red whistle hanging from his neck and says, "No. Hi. I'm an honorary brown bird." He places the whistle in his mouth, blows it, and raises his right hand with three fingers extended.
Ross holds the three-finger salute, maintaining a slight smile, then lowers his hand.

The off-screen voice asks, "What does that mean?" Ross leans closer to the door, shakes his head slightly, and says, "Well, it means that I can sell cookies, but I'm not invited to sleepovers," smiling at the end of his sentence.
Ross's smile fades as he stands upright and looks steadily at the door.
The off-screen voice says, "I can dial 911 at the press of a button, you know. Now, go away," before Ross interjects, raising his hands in a calming gesture and saying rapidly, "please. It's for"
Ross leans near the door again, continuing, "a poor little girl who wants to go to Space Camp more than anything in the world."
The off-screen voice says, "I'm pressing. A policeman is on his way." Ross straightens his posture and points toward the door.
Ross waves his hands dismissively, turns away, and says, "Okay, I'm going"
Ross walks down the hallway away from the door, bends down, and picks up a large cardboard box filled with smaller cookie boxes, turning back toward the door.
Ross stops walking. The off-screen voice says, "to I can still see you."
Ross looks back toward the peephole, says, "All right," and turns away again.
Ross continues walking down the hallway away from the door, carrying the box.
The setting changes to an outdoor Christmas tree lot at night. Joey stands near a tree holding a tool, while a blonde woman, Phoebe, wearing a red coat stands nearby.
Phoebe approaches Joey and says, "Hey."
Joey stops working, looks at her, and asks, "What are you doing here?"
Phoebe shifts her weight and says, "Well, I thought a lot about what you said and I realized maybe I was a little judgmental."
Joey rests his gloved hand on the tree as Phoebe looks around the lot.
Joey gestures toward the trees and says, "Now, Phoebe, remember hey, they're just fulfilling their Christmas..." Phoebe interjects, "...destiny," and Joey adds, "Sure."
Phoebe nods, smiles slightly, and says, "Yes."
Phoebe says, "Okay."
A man carrying a dry, brown Christmas tree walks past them toward the back of the lot.
Phoebe watches the man walk by and says, "Yikes. That one doesn't look very fulfilled."
Joey looks toward where the man carried the dry tree.
Joey turns back to Phoebe and says, "That's one of the old ones. He's just taking it to the back."
Phoebe's eyes widen as she asks, "You keep the old ones in the back? That is so ageist."
Joey shakes his head slightly.
Joey gestures with his hands, explaining, "But we have to make room for the fresh ones." Phoebe asks, "So what happens to the old guys?"
Joey shrugs and says, "Look, they go into the chipper."
Phoebe looks at Joey with a furrowed brow.
Phoebe leans her head slightly and says, "Why? I have a feeling that's not as happy as it sounds."
The man shoves the dry tree into a wood chipper. A loud grinding noise begins. Phoebe screams loudly and yells, "No."
Phoebe covers her mouth with both gloved hands, her eyes wide. Wood chips spray from the chipper's chute, and she continues screaming loudly over the grinding noise.
Phoebe turns away from the machine and buries her face into Joey's chest. Joey wraps his arms around her, his eyes wide and jaw dropped as the machine noise continues.
The setting changes to the snowy exterior of the Central Perk coffee shop, then to the interior where Ross, Joey, Phoebe, Monica, and Chandler sit around a coffee table.
Ross gestures to several boxes of cookies on the table and says, "And these come in the shapes of your favorite Christmas characters. Santa, Rudolph and Baby Jesus," holding up a box at the end.
Joey nods slightly, looking at the cookie boxes.
Joey leans forward, resting his hands on his knees, and says, "All right, I'll take a box of the cream filled Jesuses."
Ross looks at Joey, his expression tightening slightly.
Ross lowers the box he is holding and says, "Wait a minute."
Ross gestures with his hands, saying, "One box? Come on, I'm trying to send a poor little girl to Space Camp."
Ross writes on a notepad and says, "I'm putting you down for five boxes. Chandler, what about you?" Chandler asks, "All right. Do you have any coconut flavored deities?"
Ross stops writing and looks up at Chandler.
Ross picks up a blue box, shows it, and says, "No, but there's coconut in the Hanukkah Menorios."
Ross points at Chandler with his pen.
Ross resumes writing on his notepad and says, "Tell you what. I'll put you down for eight boxes. One for each night."
Chandler smiles slightly and leans his head back against the couch.
Ross turns to Monica, and asks, "Mon?"
Monica leans forward slightly, looking at Ross.
Monica says, "All right. I'll take one box of the Mint Treasures. One, and that's it," raising one finger for emphasis.
Monica gestures as she speaks, saying, "I started gaining weight after I joined the Brown Birds. Remember, dad bought every one of my boxes and I ate them all."
Ross looks at Monica, his expression neutral.
Ross says, "No, Mon. Dad had to buy every one of your boxes because you ate them," his pace slowing on the final words.
Monica looks away, pressing her lips together. Chandler glances at her, while Ross adjusts the cookie boxes on the table.
Ross holds a green box of cookies and says, "You know, I'm sure that's not going to happen this time."
Ross gestures with the green box.
Ross looks at Monica and says, "Why don't I just put you down for three of the Mint Treasures and just a couple of the Rudolphs."
In a coffee shop with holiday decorations, Monica in an orange shirt shakes her head, pointing her finger forward. "No," she says.
Ross holds a box of cookies and says, "Oh, come on now. You know you want them." Monica keeps her finger pointed.
Monica waves her hands rapidly and steps backward.
Pointing at Ross, Monica says, "Don't do this." Ross offers a green cookie box toward her, saying, "I'll tell you what, Mon. I'll give you the first box for free."
Monica waves her hands again and turns away from him.
Walking away, Monica says, "Oh, God. I gotta go."
Ross stands holding the cookie boxes, watching her depart.
Carrying a large cardboard box, Ross steps toward the seating area. "Come on. All the cool kids are eating. Oh," he calls out, looking around the room.
Ross carries the cardboard box to the exit and leaves the shop.
Gunther speaks to Rachel, who wears an apron. "and when you have a second later, I want to show you why we don't just trap spiders under coffee mugs and leave them there," he says.
Gunther walks away. Rachel presses her lips together, then walks over to an orange couch and sits between Joey and Chandler.
Rachel leans back and says, "I'm retraining to be better at a job that I hate. My life officially sucks."
Joey gestures with his hands and asks, "But, Rach, wasn't this supposed to be a"
Joey continues, "temporary thing? I thought you wanted to do fashion stuff."
Rachel adjusts her collar and replies, "Well, yeah, I'm still pursuing that."
Chandler turns to Rachel, asking, "How exactly are you pursuing"
Chandler continues, "that? Other than sending out resumes, like, what, two years ago?"
Rachel leans her head against her hand, looking away, and says, "Well, I'm also sending out"
Rachel opens her hands in a brief shrugging motion.
Rachel says, "good thoughts."
Joey leans forward, resting his arms on his legs.
Joey gestures as he speaks to Rachel. "If you ask me, as long as you got this job, you got nothing pushing you to get another one," he says.
Pointing a finger at Rachel, Joey adds, "You need the fear."
Rachel turns to Joey, her eyebrows raised.
Rachel asks, "The Fear?"
Chandler points at Joey and says, "He's right. If you quit this job, you then have motivation to go after a job you really want."
Rachel turns to Chandler, raising her hands. "Well, then how come you're still at a job that you hate? I mean, why don't you quit and get The Fear?" she asks.
Joey laughs loudly and points at Chandler. Chandler presses his lips together into a slight smile.
Chandler says, "Because I'm too afraid."
Rachel covers her face with both hands, rubbing her eyes.
Lowering her hands and gesturing outward, Rachel says, "I don't know. I mean, I would give anything to work for a designer or a buyer."
Rachel rests her head on her hand, looking down.
Rachel says, "I just don't want to be 30 and still work here."
Chandler rests a hand on his chest and replies, "Yeah, that'd be much worse than being 28 and still working here."
Chandler pats Rachel's shoulder as she keeps her head resting on her hand.
Off-screen, Gunther calls out, "Rachel?"
Rachel lifts her head and says, "Yeah?" Standing at the counter with a notepad, Gunther says, "Remind me to review with you which pot is decaf and which is regular."
Rachel closes her eyes, tilts her head back, and exhales deeply.
Rachel asks, "Can't I just look at the handles on them?"
Gunther answers, "You would think."
Rachel taps the couch armrest and stands up.
Rachel walks over to the counter, leaning her hands on it. "Okay, fine. Gunther. You know what?" she says.
Leaning further forward, Rachel continues, "I am a terrible waitress. Do you know why I am a terrible waitress? Because I don't"
Rachel says, "care. I don't care."

Gesturing emphatically with her hands, Rachel's volume rises as she says, "I don't care which pot is regular and which pot is decaf. I don't care where the tray spot is. I just don't care. This is not"
Placing her hands back on the counter, Rachel finishes, "what I want to do."
Rachel places her hands on her hips, breathing visibly as she looks at Gunther.
Rachel says, "So I don't think I should do it anymore."
Rachel maintains her stance with hands on her hips, keeping her gaze on him.
Rachel says, "I'm going to give you my week's notice."
Gunther asks, "What?" Rachel replies, "Gunther, I quit."
Gunther stares back silently.
The screen turns black.
The screen remains black.
